The Second Servant Song -- the Servant speaks in his own voice
The Fourth Servant Song -- the Servant's suffering and vindication
Jesus' baptism fulfilling 42:1 -- the Spirit descends, the Father declares delight
Matthew quotes 42:1-4 as fulfilled in Jesus' compassionate ministry
Simeon's prophecy: 'A light for revelation to the Gentiles' -- echoing 42:6 (or goyim)
Paul and Barnabas quote 'a light for the Gentiles' as their commission to preach to the nations

isaiah_comfort.divine_council_note: The First Servant Song is a divine council presentation. YHWH introduces his Servant to the assembled council: 'Behold (hen) my servant' (42:1) -- the demonstrative 'behold' presupposes an audience. The Servant receives YHWH's ruach (Spirit), marking him as the divinely empowered agent of the council's purposes. His mission -- to establish mishpat (justice) among the goyim (nations) -- directly addresses the failure condemned in Psalm 82: the elohim allotted to the nations failed to establish justice ('How long will you judge unjustly?' Ps 82:2), so YHWH's Servant will do what the council members could not. The titles berit am ('covenant for the people') and or goyim ('light for the nations') give the Servant a cosmic scope: he is not merely Israel's deliverer but the nations' hope. The Servant will 'open the eyes that are blind' (42:7) -- reversing the judicial blindness of 6:10 and 29:10 and extending sight to the nations who sit 'in darkness' (42:7b) under the governance of lesser elohim.

sections.heading: YHWH Presents His Servant (42:1-4)
sections.body: 'Hen avdi etmak-bo, bechiri ratsetah nafshi' -- 'Behold my servant whom I uphold, my chosen in whom my soul delights' (42:1a). YHWH speaks with intimate affection: nafshi ('my soul/self') delights (ratsetah, from ratsah, 'to take pleasure in, to be favorable toward'). The Spirit (ruach) is placed upon the Servant, recalling the sevenfold Spirit of 11:2 and anticipating the anointing of 61:1. The Servant's mission is mishpat la-goyim -- 'justice to the nations' (42:1b). This is not parochial but universal: the nations beyond Israel's borders will receive YHWH's justice through this Servant. The method is striking: 'He will not cry aloud (yits'aq) or lift up his voice (yissa), or make it heard in the street (chuts)' (42:2). No demagoguery, no coercion, no political grandstanding. 'A bruised reed he will not break, and a faintly burning wick he will not quench' (42:3a). The bruised reed and the dim wick represent the broken, the weak, the nearly extinguished -- and the Servant handles them with care, not contempt. Yet he is not passive: 'He will not grow faint (yikheh -- the same word as 'dim' wick) or be discouraged (yarats -- the same root as 'crushed' reed)' (42:4a). The Servant will not himself become what the people he serves are. He endures until 'he has established mishpat in the earth; and the coastlands (iyyim) wait for his torah' (42:4b).

sections.heading: The Servant's Commission: Covenant and Light (42:5-9)
sections.body: YHWH identifies himself with a creation credential: 'Thus says ha-El (the God) YHWH, who created the heavens and stretched them out, who spread out the earth and what comes from it, who gives breath (neshamah) to the people upon it and spirit (ruach) to those who walk in it' (42:5). The Creator God who sustains all life now addresses the Servant directly: 'I am YHWH; I have called you in righteousness (tsedeq); I have taken you by the hand (hechezaqtikha) and kept you (etsorka); I have given (ettenka) you as a covenant for the people (berit am), a light for the nations (or goyim)' (42:6). The Servant is not merely a covenant mediator -- he IS the covenant: berit am means the Servant embodies in his person the covenant relationship between YHWH and his people. He is also or goyim -- the light that the nations have been waiting for. His specific tasks: 'to open the eyes that are blind (ivrim), to bring out the prisoners (asirim) from the dungeon, from the prison (beit kele) those who sit in darkness (choshek)' (42:7). Blindness reversed, captives freed, darkness dispelled -- the Servant's work addresses the judicial blindness of Isaiah 6:10, the imprisonment of 24:22, and the darkness of 9:2 simultaneously. YHWH declares his exclusive deity: 'I am YHWH; that is my name; my glory (kevodi) I give to no other, nor my praise (tehillati) to carved idols' (42:8).

isaiah_comfort.title: Cyrus the Anointed: YHWH's Mashiach Among the Nations
isaiah_comfort.synopsis: In one of the most extraordinary declarations in the Hebrew Bible, YHWH calls the Persian king Cyrus by name -- a century before his rise to power -- and designates him as his 'shepherd' (ro'i, 44:28) and his 'anointed' (mashiach, 45:1). 'Thus says YHWH to his anointed (limshicho), to Cyrus (le-Koresh), whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him and to loose the belts of kings, to open doors before him that gates may not be closed' (45:1). YHWH will go before Cyrus, level the mountains, break bronze doors, and cut through iron bars (45:2). The purpose is stated: 'that you may know that it is I, YHWH, the God of Israel, who call you by your name' (45:3). And the stunning qualification: 'I call you by your name, I name you (akannekha), though you do not know me (lo yeda'tani)' (45:4). YHWH calls a pagan king who does not know him, anoints him with the title reserved for Israel's kings and priests, and uses him to accomplish his covenant purposes. The reason: 'For the sake of my servant Jacob, and Israel my chosen' (45:4). Then the monotheistic declaration: 'I am YHWH, and there is no other (ein od), besides me there is no God (elohim)... I form light and create darkness, I make peace (shalom) and create calamity (ra); I am YHWH, who does all these things' (45:5-7).

key_verse.text: Thus says the LORD to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him and to loose the belts of kings, to open doors before him that gates may not be closed.

hebrew_terms: mashiach (anointed one -- the title applied to Cyrus, normally reserved for Israel's kings and priests)
Koresh (Cyrus -- the Persian king named by God before his birth)
ro'i (my shepherd -- YHWH's pastoral title for the pagan king who will free Israel)
ein od (there is no other -- the absolute monotheistic formula)
yotser or (former of light -- YHWH as creator of all reality, light and darkness)
bore ra (creator of calamity -- YHWH's sovereignty over adversity as well as blessing)
isaiah_comfort.ane_backdrop: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC, now in the British Museum) records Cyrus' own account of his conquest of Babylon. Cyrus claims that Marduk, the chief god of Babylon, selected him and 'called him by name' to restore the displaced peoples and their gods to their homelands. The parallel to Isaiah 45 is striking: both texts describe a deity calling Cyrus by name and commissioning him for conquest and restoration. The critical difference is theological: Isaiah claims it is YHWH, not Marduk, who truly called Cyrus -- and that Cyrus does not even know the God who appointed him. The application of the title mashiach to a pagan king is unprecedented in the Hebrew Bible and demonstrates YHWH's absolute sovereignty: he can anoint whomever he chooses, regardless of their religious affiliation.

second_temple.summary: Josephus claims that Cyrus read the Isaiah prophecy about himself and was moved to fulfill it by decreeing the Jews' return: 'This was known to Cyrus by his reading the book which Isaiah left behind him of his prophecies.'
second_temple.relevance: Whether historically accurate or not, Josephus' account shows that Second Temple Judaism understood Isaiah 44-45 as a prophecy specifically about Cyrus, given by God generations before its fulfillment.

second_temple.summary: Cyrus' decree releasing the Jews: 'YHWH, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and he has charged me to build him a house at Jerusalem.'
second_temple.relevance: The Ezra account echoes the Isaiah prophecy: Cyrus acknowledges (at least formally) that YHWH has commissioned him.

cross_refs.note: Cyrus' decree fulfilling the word of YHWH spoken through Jeremiah
Cyrus' decree releasing the Jews -- the fulfillment of Isaiah 44:28-45:1
The Prince of Persia as a territorial spirit -- Cyrus operates in a realm governed by a spiritual power, yet YHWH overrides it
YHWH using Assyria as 'the rod of my anger' -- the same pattern of using pagan rulers as divine instruments
Paul's argument about God's sovereignty over vessels of wrath and mercy -- the Cyrus principle applied broadly
'The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of YHWH; he turns it wherever he will'

isaiah_comfort.divine_council_note: The Cyrus oracle is a divine council decree of extraordinary scope. YHWH, the Most High (Elyon) who allotted nations to the sons of God (Deut 32:8-9), now reaches into Persia -- a nation under the governance of a territorial spirit (the 'Prince of Persia' in Daniel 10:13, 20) -- and anoints its king as his instrument. The title mashiach (anointed one) had been reserved for Israel's kings (1 Sam 24:6, 10; 2 Sam 1:14) and priests (Lev 4:3, 5, 16). To apply it to a pagan king demonstrates that YHWH's sovereignty is not bounded by the Deuteronomy 32 allotment. He can commission agents from any nation, override any territorial spirit's authority, and accomplish his purposes through rulers who do not even know him. The monotheistic formula 'I am YHWH, and there is no other' (ein od, 45:5-6) is not a denial that other elohim exist (the divine council is repeatedly affirmed) but a declaration that no other elohim possesses YHWH's sovereign authority. He alone forms light, creates darkness, makes peace, and creates calamity (45:7). The Cyrus oracle is YHWH demonstrating to his own council, to the territorial spirits, and to the nations that his sovereignty is absolute.

sections.heading: Cyrus Named as YHWH's Shepherd and Anointed (44:28-45:1)
sections.body: The oracle begins at the end of chapter 44: YHWH 'says of Cyrus (Koresh), He is my shepherd (ro'i), and he shall fulfill all my purpose (chephtsi); saying of Jerusalem, She shall be built, and of the temple, Your foundation shall be laid' (44:28). The title ro'i ('my shepherd') is the pastoral image applied to David (2 Sam 5:2; Ps 78:70-72) and to YHWH himself (Ps 23:1). Its application to a Persian king who worships Ahura Mazda is theologically revolutionary. Chapter 45 opens with the full commissioning: 'Thus says YHWH to his mashiach (limshicho), to Cyrus (le-Koresh), whose right hand I have grasped (hechezaqti bimino)' (45:1). The right hand grasp is the gesture of coronation and empowerment -- YHWH personally installs Cyrus as his agent. The military commission: 'to subdue nations before him and to loose the belts (motnei) of kings' (45:1b) -- loosing the belt was a symbol of disarming and deposing. 'To open doors (delatayim) before him that gates (she'arim) may not be closed' (45:1c) -- possibly a reference to Babylon's gates, which ancient tradition says were left open when Cyrus entered the city in 539 BC.

sections.heading: I Am YHWH and There Is No Other (45:2-7)
sections.body: YHWH describes what he will do for Cyrus: 'I will go before you and level the mountains (hadurim); I will break in pieces the doors of bronze (dalethot nechoshet) and cut through the bars of iron (berichei varzel)' (45:2). The language is of siege warfare, but YHWH does the breaching. The purpose: 'that you may know (teda) that it is I, YHWH, the God of Israel, who call you by your name (be-shimkha)' (45:3b). To 'call by name' is to have authority over -- YHWH's naming of Cyrus establishes divine sovereignty over the Persian king. The paradox is stated plainly: 'I name you, though you do not know me (lo yeda'tani)' (45:4b). Cyrus does not know YHWH -- yet YHWH knows, names, and uses Cyrus. 'I equip you (a'azerka), though you do not know me' (45:5b). Then the absolute monotheistic declaration: 'I am YHWH, and there is no other (ein od). Besides me there is no elohim' (45:5a). And the comprehensive claim of sovereignty: 'I form (yotser) light (or) and create (bore) darkness (choshek); I make (oseh) peace (shalom) and create (bore) calamity (ra); I am YHWH, who does all these things' (45:7). This is not dualism but absolute monergism: YHWH is the source of both light and darkness, both shalom and ra. No power in the universe operates outside his sovereignty.

isaiah_comfort.title: The Second Servant Song: Called from the Womb, Light to the Nations
isaiah_comfort.synopsis: The Servant speaks in his own voice for the first time. 'Listen to me, O coastlands (iyyim), and give attention, you peoples from afar (le'ummim me-rachoq). YHWH called me from the womb (mibeten); from the body of my mother (mimme'ei immi) he named me' (49:1). The Servant's mouth is 'like a sharp sword' (cherev chaddah) and he is 'a polished arrow' (chets barar) hidden in YHWH's quiver (49:2). YHWH says to him: 'You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified (etpa'ar)' (49:3). The Servant is named 'Israel' -- yet he has a mission to Israel: 'to bring Jacob back to him... to raise up the tribes of Jacob and to restore the preserved (netsurei) of Israel' (49:5-6). The Servant is Israel's representative and yet distinct from Israel -- he does what Israel failed to do. Then YHWH expands the mission: 'It is too light a thing (naqal) that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob... I will make you as a light for the nations (or goyim), that my salvation (yeshu'ati) may reach to the end of the earth' (49:6). The Servant is not merely Israel's restorer but the world's savior. The chapter then describes the Servant as 'deeply despised (bazoh nefesh), abhorred by the nation (meta'ev goy)' (49:7) -- foreshadowing the suffering that will be fully described in the Fourth Song.

key_verse.text: He says: 'It is too light a thing that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob and to bring back the preserved of Israel; I will make you as a light for the nations, that my salvation may reach to the end of the earth.'

hebrew_terms: mibeten (from the womb -- the Servant's calling precedes his birth)
cherev chaddah (sharp sword -- the Servant's mouth as a weapon of divine speech)
chets barar (polished arrow -- hidden in YHWH's quiver, deployed at the right moment)
or goyim (light for the nations -- the universal scope of the Servant's mission)
yeshu'ah (salvation -- YHWH's rescue reaching the earth's ends through the Servant)
naqal (too light/trivial -- the Servant's mission exceeds even Israel's restoration)
isaiah_comfort.ane_backdrop: The concept of a divinely called servant whose mission extends beyond his own nation is unusual in the ANE, where the gods' interests were typically identified with their own people's welfare. The Servant's prenatal calling parallels the Mesopotamian royal birth narratives where gods select a king before birth (cf. Ashurbanipal's claim that Ashur and Sin 'called my name for the rule of Assyria' while he was still in his mother's womb).

second_temple.summary: Simeon's prophecy over the infant Jesus: 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to your people Israel.'
second_temple.relevance: Simeon quotes Isaiah 49:6, identifying Jesus as the Servant who is both Israel's glory and the Gentiles' light.

second_temple.summary: Paul and Barnabas declare: 'For so the Lord has commanded us, saying, I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.'
second_temple.relevance: The apostolic mission to the Gentiles is understood as fulfilling the Second Servant Song -- the church participates in the Servant's mission.

cross_refs.note: The First Servant Song -- the Servant introduced by YHWH; now the Servant speaks
'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you' -- the same prenatal calling pattern
Paul says God 'set me apart before I was born and called me by his grace' -- echoing the Servant's calling
Simeon identifies Jesus as the 'light for the Gentiles' of 49:6
The apostolic mission as fulfillment of the or goyim (light to the nations) commission
'The word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword' -- echoing the Servant's sword-mouth
'From his mouth came a sharp two-edged sword' -- Christ as the Servant with the sword-mouth

isaiah_comfort.divine_council_note: The Second Servant Song expands the Servant's mission beyond the scope of any previous biblical commission. YHWH declares that restoring Israel is 'too light a thing' (naqal) -- the Servant's task extends to the nations. This directly addresses the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 situation: YHWH kept Israel as his nachalah while allotting other nations to the sons of God. Now the Servant will be the agent through whom YHWH's yeshu'ah ('salvation') reaches 'the end of the earth' (49:6). The Servant is YHWH's means of reclaiming the nations from the governance of the lesser elohim. The Servant being 'deeply despised' and 'abhorred by the nation' (49:7) anticipates the paradox of the Fourth Song: the agent of universal salvation will be rejected by the very people he comes to save.

sections.heading: The Servant Speaks: Called from the Womb (49:1-3)
sections.body: For the first time, the Servant addresses the nations directly: 'Listen to me, O coastlands (iyyim)... YHWH called me from the womb (mibeten); from the body of my mother he named me (hizkir shemi)' (49:1). The calling precedes birth -- the Servant was designated before he existed, like Jeremiah (Jer 1:5) and like Cyrus (Isa 45:4). YHWH equipped him: 'He made my mouth like a sharp sword (cherev chaddah)' (49:2a) -- speech as weapon. 'In the shadow of his hand he hid me; he made me a polished arrow (chets barar); in his quiver (ashpatho) he hid me away' (49:2b). The Servant is a concealed weapon -- prepared, sharpened, hidden until the moment of deployment. YHWH identifies the Servant: 'You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified (etpa'ar)' (49:3). The name 'Israel' creates the central paradox of the Servant Songs: the Servant is identified with Israel and yet has a mission to Israel (49:5-6). He is the ideal Israel -- the one who fulfills what the nation as a whole could not.

sections.heading: A Light to the Nations: Beyond Israel's Restoration (49:4-7)
sections.body: The Servant expresses apparent frustration: 'I have labored in vain (lariq yaga'ti); I have spent my strength for nothing and vanity (lethohw vehevel)' (49:4a). Yet he immediately trusts: 'Surely my mishpat (justice/cause) is with YHWH, and my recompense (pe'ullati) with my God' (49:4b). YHWH responds by expanding the mission: 'It is too light a thing (naqal) that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob and to restore the preserved of Israel; I will make you as a light for the nations (or goyim), that my salvation (yeshu'ati) may reach to the end (qetseh) of the earth' (49:6). The scope is breathtaking: the Servant's mission extends from Israel to the entire human race. YHWH then addresses the political dimension: 'Thus says YHWH, the Redeemer (Go'el) of Israel and his Holy One (Qedesho), to one deeply despised (bazoh-nefesh), abhorred by the nation (meta'ev goy), the servant of rulers: Kings shall see and arise; princes, and they shall prostrate themselves' (49:7). The despised Servant will be vindicated: the rulers of the earth will bow before the one they rejected.

isaiah_comfort.title: The Third Servant Song: The Obedient Disciple Who Does Not Turn Back
isaiah_comfort.synopsis: The Third Servant Song reveals the Servant's inner life of obedience and suffering. 'The Lord YHWH (Adonai YHWH) has given me the tongue of those who are taught (leshon limmudim), that I may know how to sustain with a word (davar) him who is weary (ya'ef). Morning by morning (babboqer babboqer) he awakens; he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught' (50:4). The Servant is a disciple before he is a teacher -- he listens to YHWH every morning. 'The Lord YHWH has opened my ear, and I was not rebellious (lo mariti); I turned not backward (lo nesugoti)' (50:5). Then the suffering: 'I gave my back (gevi) to those who strike (makkim), and my cheeks (lechayai) to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace (kelimmot) and spitting (roq)' (50:6). The Servant submits to physical abuse and humiliation voluntarily -- he 'gave' his back, he did not merely endure. His confidence is in YHWH: 'The Lord YHWH helps me; therefore I have not been disgraced... I have set my face like a flint (challamish), and I know that I shall not be put to shame (evosh)' (50:7). The legal challenge follows: 'He who vindicates me (matsdiqqi) is near. Who will contend (yariv) with me?... Who is my adversary (ba'al mishpati)? Let him come near to me' (50:8). The Servant stands in a courtroom, but with YHWH as his advocate, no accuser can prevail.

key_verse.text: I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting.

hebrew_terms: leshon limmudim (tongue of the taught/disciples -- the Servant learns before he speaks)
babboqer babboqer (morning by morning -- the daily discipline of divine instruction)
lo mariti (I was not rebellious -- the Servant's perfect obedience, unlike Israel)
gevi (my back -- given voluntarily to those who strike)
kelimmot varoq (disgrace and spitting -- the shame the Servant willingly endures)
challamish (flint -- the hardness of resolved obedience, not stubbornness)
matsdiqqi (the one who vindicates me -- YHWH as the Servant's legal defender)
isaiah_comfort.ane_backdrop: The description of the Servant's suffering -- striking the back, pulling out the beard, spitting in the face -- matches known ANE humiliation practices. In Mesopotamian legal and military contexts, these were acts of public shaming reserved for defeated enemies, rebellious slaves, and condemned criminals. Pulling out the beard was particularly degrading in cultures where the beard was a symbol of honor and masculinity. The Servant's voluntary acceptance of these indignities is without parallel in ANE literature.

second_temple.summary: 'Then they spit in his face and struck him. And some slapped him.'
second_temple.relevance: The Passion narrative directly fulfills the Third Servant Song's description of striking, spitting, and disgrace.

second_temple.summary: 'He set his face to go to Jerusalem' -- using language that echoes the Servant's 'I have set my face like a flint' (50:7).
second_temple.relevance: Luke deliberately echoes the Third Song's language of resolved obedience when describing Jesus' determination to face his death in Jerusalem.

cross_refs.note: Jesus struck, spit upon, and mocked -- fulfilling the Third Song's suffering description
Jesus 'set his face to go to Jerusalem' -- echoing 50:7, 'I have set my face like a flint'
'And some began to spit on him and to cover his face and to strike him' -- the Third Song fulfilled
'Who shall bring any charge against God's elect?... Who is to condemn?' -- Paul echoing the courtroom challenge of 50:8-9
The First Song's gentle Servant now reveals the cost of his gentleness: suffering
The Fourth Song will fully reveal what the Third Song hints: redemptive suffering
'I am a worm... scorned by mankind and despised by the people' -- the same Servant suffering pattern

isaiah_comfort.divine_council_note: The Third Servant Song introduces the courtroom dimension of the Servant's experience. The language of vindication (matsdiqqi), contention (yariv), and adversary (ba'al mishpat) is forensic -- the Servant stands before a tribunal. YHWH is his advocate: 'He who vindicates me is near' (50:8). This anticipates the divine council trial scene implied in 52:13-53:12, where the Servant's suffering is judicially decreed and his vindication divinely guaranteed. The Servant's confidence that 'the Lord YHWH helps me' (50:7, 9) is not naive optimism but a conviction rooted in the divine council's decree: the Servant suffers according to plan, and the plan includes vindication.

sections.heading: The Servant's Daily Discipline and Perfect Obedience (50:4-5)
sections.body: 'Adonai YHWH has given me leshon limmudim (the tongue of the taught)' (50:4a). The Servant is first a disciple (limmud) before he is a speaker. The 'tongue of the taught' means his speech originates not in his own wisdom but in what he has received from YHWH. The purpose: 'that I may know how to sustain (la'ut) with a word (davar) him who is weary (ya'ef)' (50:4b). The Servant's speech is restorative, aimed at the exhausted and broken. The learning is daily: 'Morning by morning (babboqer babboqer) he awakens; he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught (limmudim)' (50:4c). Every dawn, YHWH opens the Servant's ear -- the daily rhythm of revelation and receptivity. 'Adonai YHWH has opened my ear, and I was not rebellious (lo mariti); I turned not backward (lo nesugoti)' (50:5). The double negation emphasizes the Servant's perfect obedience: he does not rebel (like Israel repeatedly did) and he does not retreat (as a coward would). This perfect obedience is the precondition for the suffering that follows.

sections.heading: Voluntary Suffering and Flint-Faced Resolve (50:6-9)
sections.body: 'I gave (natati) my back to those who strike (makkim), and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard' (50:6a). The key word is natati ('I gave') -- active, voluntary surrender. The Servant is not overpowered but willingly endures. The pulling of the beard is one of the most degrading acts in the ancient world -- it strips a man of his dignity and honor. 'I hid not (lo histarti) my face from disgrace (kelimmot) and spitting (roq)' (50:6b). Spitting in the face is the ultimate expression of contempt (Num 12:14; Deut 25:9). The Servant does not hide or flinch. His confidence: 'Adonai YHWH helps me (ya'azor-li); therefore I have not been disgraced (niklamti); therefore I have set my face like flint (challamish)' (50:7). Flint is the hardest common stone in the Levant -- the Servant's resolve is mineral, unyielding. The forensic challenge: 'He who vindicates me (matsdiqqi) is near (qarov). Who will contend (yariv) with me? Let us stand together! Who is ba'al mishpati (my adversary/accuser)? Let him come near to me!' (50:8). The Servant dares anyone to bring a case against him in the divine court. The conclusion: 'Behold, Adonai YHWH helps me; who will declare me guilty (yarshi'eni)?' (50:9). With YHWH as advocate, the verdict is certain: the Servant will be acquitted.

isaiah_comfort.title: The Fourth Servant Song: The Suffering Servant -- Pierced, Crushed, Healed
isaiah_comfort.synopsis: The theological summit of the Hebrew Bible. The Fourth Servant Song, spanning 52:13-53:12, is the most detailed messianic prophecy in the Old Testament and the most extensive treatment of redemptive suffering in all of Scripture. It begins with YHWH's declaration: 'Behold, my servant shall deal wisely (yaskil); he shall be high (yarum) and lifted up (venissa) and exalted (vegavah me'od)' (52:13) -- the same language used for YHWH enthroned in 6:1, now applied to the Servant. Yet his appearance was 'marred beyond human semblance (mishchat me'ish mar'ehu), and his form beyond that of the children of mankind' (52:14). 'He was despised (nivzeh) and rejected by men (va-chadal ishim), a man of sorrows (ish makh'ovot) and acquainted with grief (yedua choli)' (53:3). The confession of the speakers (Israel? the nations?) is devastating: 'Surely he has borne our griefs (cholayenu) and carried our sorrows (makh'oveinu); yet we esteemed him stricken, smitten by God, and afflicted' (53:4). 'He was pierced (mecholal) for our transgressions (pesha'einu); he was crushed (medukka) for our iniquities (avonotenu); upon him was the chastisement (musar) that brought us peace (shelomenu), and by his wounds (chaburatho) we are healed (nirpa-lanu)' (53:5). 'YHWH has laid on him (hiphgia bo) the iniquity (avon) of us all' (53:6). He is led 'like a lamb (seh) to the slaughter (tevach)' (53:7), 'cut off from the land of the living (erets chayyim)' (53:8), assigned a grave with the wicked and the rich in his death (53:9). Yet 'it was the will (chephets) of YHWH to crush (dakke'o) him' (53:10). His soul is made 'an offering for guilt (asham)' (53:10) -- the technical term from the Levitical sacrificial system. He 'shall see his offspring (zar'o); he shall prolong his days (ya'arikh yamim)' (53:10) -- life after death. 'By his knowledge (beda'to) shall the righteous one (tsaddiq), my servant, make many to be accounted righteous (yatsdiq), and he shall bear their iniquities (avonotam yisbol)' (53:11). YHWH concludes: 'I will divide him a portion with the many (rabbim)... because he poured out his soul (nafesho) to death (lammavet) and was numbered with the transgressors (posh'im); yet he bore the sin of many (chet-rabbim), and makes intercession (yaphgia) for the transgressors' (53:12).

key_verse.text: But he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed.

hebrew_terms: mecholal (pierced/wounded -- from the root chalal, to bore through, pierce fatally)
medukka (crushed -- from the root daka, to be broken to pieces, pulverized)
musar (chastisement/discipline -- the corrective punishment the Servant bears for others)
chaburah (wound/welt/stripe -- the physical marks of beating that bring healing)
asham (guilt offering -- the Levitical sacrifice for trespass; the Servant's soul is the offering)
yaskil (deal wisely/prosper -- the same word as in Joshua 1:8; also 'to cause to understand')
nivzeh (despised -- treated with contempt, regarded as worthless)
ish makh'ovot (man of sorrows/pains -- one whose identity is defined by suffering)
seh (lamb -- the sacrificial animal led to slaughter; the Passover lamb image)
tsaddiq (righteous one -- the Servant's character that enables substitutionary atonement)
isaiah_comfort.ane_backdrop: Substitutionary suffering has antecedents in ANE ritual practice. The Mesopotamian 'substitute king' (shar puhi) ritual involved placing a commoner on the throne during an eclipse (considered an omen of the king's death), then killing the substitute so the real king could live. The Hittite 'scapegoat' ritual (the azazel-parallel) sent an animal into the wilderness carrying the community's impurities. The Israelite Yom Kippur ritual (Leviticus 16) combined both: one goat was slaughtered as a sin offering, and the other (the scapegoat, azazel) was sent away bearing the people's sins. Isaiah 53 transcends all ANE antecedents: the substitute is not an animal or an unwitting commoner but a righteous person who voluntarily bears the sins of the many.

second_temple.summary: The Ethiopian eunuch reads Isaiah 53:7-8 and asks Philip: 'About whom does the prophet say this, about himself or about someone else?' Philip 'beginning with this Scripture told him the good news about Jesus.'
second_temple.relevance: This is the earliest recorded instance of Isaiah 53 being applied to Jesus -- the apostolic interpretation of the Suffering Servant as Christ.

second_temple.summary: Peter quotes Isaiah 53 extensively: 'He committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth... He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree... By his wounds you have been healed.'
second_temple.relevance: Peter applies the Fourth Song directly to Christ's crucifixion, interpreting the cross as the fulfillment of the Servant's substitutionary suffering.

second_temple.summary: Christ 'was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification' -- echoing Isaiah 53:5 (pierced for our transgressions) and 53:11 (the righteous one making many righteous).
second_temple.relevance: Paul's atonement theology is built on the Fourth Servant Song's framework of substitutionary suffering and vindication.

second_temple.summary: The Targum identifies the Servant as the Messiah but reinterprets the suffering: it is the nations, not the Messiah, who suffer. The Targum reads 53:3 as 'he will be for the contempt of all kingdoms' rather than 'despised and rejected by men.'
second_temple.relevance: The Targumic reinterpretation shows that post-Christian rabbinic Judaism maintained the messianic identification but redirected the suffering away from the Messiah himself -- evidence that the text was a point of controversy between Jews and Christians.

second_temple.summary: The complete Qumran Isaiah scroll includes a fascinating variant in 52:14: 'I anointed (mashachti) his appearance beyond that of any man' -- implying the Servant's disfigurement was a form of anointing.
second_temple.relevance: This Qumran variant may reflect a pre-Christian tradition connecting the Servant's suffering to his messianic (mashiach) identity.

cross_refs.note: 'High and lifted up' (ram venissa) -- the same language for YHWH enthroned is used for the Servant in 52:13
The asham (guilt offering) -- the sacrificial category assigned to the Servant's death in 53:10
The scapegoat bearing the people's iniquities -- the Servant bears iniquities (avon) in 53:6, 11
The suffering righteous one -- pierced hands and feet, garments divided, yet vindicated
'Those who turn many to righteousness (matsdiqei ha-rabbim)' -- echoing the Servant who 'makes many righteous' (yatsdiq... rabbim)
'He took our illnesses and bore our diseases' -- quoting Isaiah 53:4
'The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' -- the Servant's mission statement
Philip identifies the Suffering Servant as Jesus for the Ethiopian eunuch
'By the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous' -- the Servant who makes many righteous
Peter's extensive quotation of Isaiah 53 applied to Christ's cross
Christ 'emptied himself... humbled himself... therefore God has highly exalted him' -- the descent-and-exaltation pattern of the Fourth Song
'A Lamb standing, as though it had been slain' -- the Servant-as-Lamb of 53:7 enthroned in heaven
'Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many (pollon)' -- the author of Hebrews directly develops 53:12 ('he bore the sin of many') into a full priestly-sacrificial Christology
'Behold, the Lamb of God (ho amnos tou theou) who takes away the sin of the world!' -- John the Baptist identifies Jesus as the Servant-Lamb of 53:7
The Son of Man receiving dominion 'that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him' -- the exalted figure of 52:13-15 who causes kings to shut their mouths
The Messiah at YHWH's right hand, a priest forever -- the Servant of 52:13 who is 'high and lifted up' and who sprinkles (yazzeh) the nations in priestly purification (52:15)
'My servant the Branch... I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day' -- the priestly servant who removes sin, paralleling 53:11

isaiah_comfort.divine_council_note: The Fourth Servant Song is the divine council's most extraordinary decree. YHWH himself announces the plan (52:13-15): the Servant will be exalted to the highest position -- 'high and lifted up' (yarum venissa), the language of 6:1 where YHWH sits on his throne. The same vocabulary used for the divine King enthroned in the council chamber is applied to the suffering Servant. This is the two-powers-in-heaven theology at its most concentrated: the figure who occupies YHWH's throne position (52:13) is also the one 'marred beyond human semblance' (52:14), 'despised and rejected' (53:3), 'pierced for our transgressions' (53:5), and 'cut off from the land of the living' (53:8). The decree that 'it was the will (chephets) of YHWH to crush him' (53:10) means the suffering is not accidental but deliberate -- planned in the divine council and executed according to divine purpose. The Servant's soul is made an asham (guilt offering) -- the council transforms the Levitical sacrificial system from animal substitution to personal substitution. The righteous one (tsaddiq) bears the sins of the many (rabbim) and 'makes intercession (yaphgia) for the transgressors' (53:12) -- functioning as both sacrifice and priest, both victim and intercessor, both the one who dies and the one who 'prolongs his days' (53:10) after death. No passage in the Hebrew Bible more fully anticipates the New Testament proclamation of the cross and resurrection.

sections.heading: YHWH's Introduction: Exalted, Yet Marred (52:13-15)
sections.body: 'Behold, my servant shall deal wisely (yaskil); he shall be high (yarum) and lifted up (venissa) and exalted (vegavah me'od)' (52:13). The three verbs of exaltation match the three verbs of abasement that follow. The word yaskil can mean 'to prosper' or 'to act wisely' -- the same word used for the blessed man in Joshua 1:8 and Psalm 1. But the exaltation comes through horror: 'As many were astonished (shamemu) at you -- his appearance was so marred (mishchat), beyond human semblance (me'ish), and his form beyond that of the children of mankind (mibbenei adam)' (52:14). The Servant is beaten so severely that he no longer looks human. 'So shall he sprinkle (yazzeh) many nations' (52:15a) -- the verb nazah is the technical term for priestly sprinkling of blood or water for ritual purification (Lev 4:6; 14:7; 16:14-15). The Servant's suffering accomplishes priestly purification for the nations. 'Kings shall shut their mouths because of him, for that which has not been told them they see, and that which they have not heard they understand' (52:15b) -- kings, the most powerful people on earth, are silenced by the revelation of the Servant.

sections.heading: The Confession: Our Griefs, Our Sorrows, Our Healing (53:1-6)
sections.body: 'Who has believed what he has heard from us (lishmu'atenu)? And to whom has the arm of YHWH (zeroa YHWH) been revealed?' (53:1). The 'arm of YHWH' is the divine warrior's combat power (Exod 6:6; 15:16; Deut 4:34; Isa 40:10; 51:9). The Servant IS the arm of YHWH -- the divine warrior's power made manifest not in military conquest but in suffering love. 'He grew up before him like a young plant (yoneq), and like a root (shoresh) out of dry ground (erets tsiyyah)' (53:2a). The messianic 'branch' imagery of 11:1 reappears -- but now the branch grows from parched soil, not from a stump. 'He had no form (to'ar) or majesty (hadar) that we should look at him, and no beauty (mar'eh) that we should desire him' (53:2b). 'He was despised (nivzeh) and rejected by men (chadal ishim), a man of sorrows (ish makh'ovot) and acquainted with grief (yedua choli); and as one from whom men hide their faces (mahster panim) he was despised, and we esteemed him not (lo chashavnuhu)' (53:3). Then the confession: 'Surely he has borne our griefs (cholayenu hu nasa) and carried our sorrows (umakh'oveinu sevalam)' (53:4a). The verbs nasa ('to bear, carry') and saval ('to carry a heavy load') are the vocabulary of burden-bearing. 'He was pierced (mecholal) for our transgressions (pesha'einu); he was crushed (medukka) for our iniquities (avonotenu); upon him was the musar (chastisement/discipline) that brought us shalom (shelomenu), and by his chaburah (wound/stripe) we are healed (nirpa-lanu)' (53:5). Every key word is substitutionary: our transgressions, his piercing; our iniquities, his crushing; our peace, his punishment; our healing, his wounds. 'All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned -- every one -- to his own way; and YHWH has laid on him (hiphgia bo) the iniquity (avon) of us all' (53:6). The verb hiphgia (from paga, 'to cause to meet, to lay upon') means YHWH actively placed the sin burden on the Servant.

sections.heading: The Lamb, the Grave, and the Divine Will (53:7-10)
sections.body: 'He was oppressed (niggas) and he was afflicted (na'aneh), yet he opened not his mouth (lo yiptach-piv); like a lamb (seh) that is led to the slaughter (tevach), and like a sheep (rachel) that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth' (53:7). The double silence -- he opened not his mouth -- emphasizes the Servant's willing submission. The lamb imagery connects to the Passover lamb (Exod 12:3-6), the daily tamid offering (Exod 29:38-42), and the sin offering (Lev 4). 'By oppression and judgment (me'otser umimmishpat) he was taken away' (53:8a) -- a legal proceeding precedes his death. 'And as for his generation, who considered that he was cut off (nigzar) from the land of the living (erets chayyim), stricken for the transgression (pesha) of my people (ammi)?' (53:8b). The Servant dies -- 'cut off from the land of the living' is a euphemism for death. 'And they made his grave with the wicked (resha'im) and with a rich man (ashir) in his death (bemotav)' (53:9a). The grave assigned with criminals, yet actually with the wealthy -- an apparent contradiction fulfilled in Jesus' burial in the rich man Joseph of Arimathea's tomb (Matt 27:57-60). 'Although he had done no violence (chamas), and there was no deceit (mirmah) in his mouth' (53:9b) -- his death was not punishment for his own sin. 'Yet it was the will (chephets) of YHWH to crush him (dakke'o); he has put him to grief (hechelih); when his soul makes an offering for guilt (asham)' (53:10a). The asham is the guilt/trespass offering of Leviticus 5:14-6:7 -- the sacrifice for offenses against sacred things. The Servant's soul (nafesho) IS the asham.

sections.heading: Life After Death: Offspring, Days, Intercession (53:10b-12)
sections.body: After death, something impossible happens: 'he shall see his offspring (zar'o); he shall prolong his days (ya'arikh yamim)' (53:10b). A man who was 'cut off from the land of the living' now sees descendants and has extended days -- this is life after death, resurrection implied though not yet explicit. 'The will (chephets) of YHWH shall prosper (yitslach) in his hand' (53:10c) -- the divine purpose succeeds through the risen Servant. 'Out of the anguish (amal) of his soul he shall see and be satisfied (yisba). By his knowledge (beda'to) shall the righteous one (tsaddiq), my servant, make many to be accounted righteous (yatsdiq larabbim), and he shall bear their iniquities (avonotam yisbol)' (53:11). The Servant accomplishes justification -- he makes the many (rabbim) righteous. The mechanism is substitutionary: he bears their iniquities. YHWH's final declaration: 'Therefore I will divide him a portion with the many (rabbim), and he shall divide the spoil with the strong (atsumim), because (tachat asher) he poured out his soul (nafesho) to death (lammavet) and was numbered with the transgressors (posh'im); yet he bore the sin of many (chet-rabbim nasa), and makes intercession (yaphgia) for the transgressors (posh'im)' (53:12). The Servant receives the victor's reward -- a portion with the mighty -- not because he conquered by force but because he conquered by sacrifice. He poured out his life, was counted among sinners, bore their sin, and continues to intercede. The present tense of yaphgia ('makes intercession') suggests ongoing mediation -- the Servant who suffered and rose continues to advocate for transgressors.

isaiah_comfort.title: Come to the Waters: The Everlasting Covenant
isaiah_comfort.synopsis: The closing chapter of Second Isaiah issues a universal invitation: 'Come, everyone who thirsts (tsame), come to the waters; and he who has no money (kesef), come, buy and eat! Come, buy wine and milk without money and without price (mechlr)' (55:1). The gospel invitation is free -- salvation cannot be purchased, only received. YHWH promises 'an everlasting covenant (berit olam), my steadfast, sure love for David (chasdei David hanne'emanim)' (55:3). The Davidic covenant, which seemed dead after the exile destroyed the monarchy, is renewed and universalized. YHWH's word is guaranteed: 'For as the rain and the snow come down from heaven and do not return there but water the earth... so shall my word (devari) be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty (reqam), but it shall accomplish that which I purpose (chaphatseti), and shall succeed in the thing for which I sent it' (55:10-11). The chapter, and Second Isaiah, ends with a cosmic exodus: 'For you shall go out in joy (simchah) and be led forth in peace (shalom); the mountains and the hills before you shall break forth into singing, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands' (55:12).

key_verse.text: So shall my word be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose, and shall succeed in the thing for which I sent it.

hebrew_terms: tsame (thirsty -- spiritual need described as physical thirst)
berit olam (everlasting covenant -- the Davidic covenant renewed and made permanent)
chasdei David (the faithful loves of David -- YHWH's covenant mercies promised to David)
devar YHWH (word of YHWH -- the speech-act that accomplishes its purpose)
reqam (empty/in vain -- the word of YHWH never returns fruitless)
chaphets (purpose/delight -- the divine will that the word executes)
isaiah_comfort.ane_backdrop: Royal banquets offered freely to the public were known in ANE court practice -- kings would celebrate military victories or accession to the throne with public feasts. The free invitation of Isaiah 55 transforms the royal banquet tradition: YHWH the King offers a feast to all who thirst, regardless of social standing or wealth. The emphasis on 'without money and without price' (55:1) subverts the ANE economic system where everything, including divine favor (through temple offerings), had a price.

second_temple.summary: Jesus cries out: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink' -- echoing the water invitation of Isaiah 55:1.
second_temple.relevance: Jesus identifies himself as the source of the 'waters' that Isaiah 55 invites the thirsty to come and drink.

second_temple.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 55:3 to support the resurrection: 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David.'
second_temple.relevance: Paul reads the 'sure mercies of David' as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection -- the everlasting covenant is ratified by the risen Messiah.

cross_refs.note: 'With joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation' -- the same water-of-salvation imagery
The Davidic covenant that 55:3 renews as an 'everlasting covenant'
Jesus as the living water, fulfilling the invitation of 55:1
Paul connects 55:3 to Christ's resurrection
'Faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ' -- the effectual word of 55:11
'Let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who desires take the water of life without price' -- the final echo of Isaiah 55:1

isaiah_comfort.divine_council_note: Isaiah 55 closes Second Isaiah with a declaration of YHWH's word as the supreme force in the universe. 'My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways' (55:8) -- YHWH's counsel (from the divine council) transcends all human understanding. The effectual word (55:10-11) that accomplishes its purpose is the same dabar (word) that created the heavens (Ps 33:6), the same decree that the divine council issues, the same speech-act by which YHWH governs history. The renewal of the Davidic covenant (55:3) as an everlasting covenant (berit olam) universalizes what was once a national promise: the 'sure mercies of David' are now available to 'everyone who thirsts' (55:1), regardless of national origin. The Deuteronomy 32 boundaries between Israel and the nations begin to dissolve.

sections.heading: The Universal Invitation: Come, Buy Without Price (55:1-5)
sections.body: 'Ho (hoi), everyone who thirsts (tsame), come to the waters (mayim); and he who has no money (kesef), come, buy (shivru) and eat! Come, buy wine (yayin) and milk (chalav) without money (kesef) and without price (mechir)' (55:1). The invitation subverts the economic order: the most valuable goods -- water, wine, milk -- are offered freely. 'Why do you spend your money for that which is not bread (lechem), and your labor for that which does not satisfy (sove'ah)?' (55:2). YHWH offers 'an everlasting covenant, my steadfast, sure mercies for David (chasdei David hanne'emanim)' (55:3). The Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7 is renewed, but the recipient is now universalized: 'Behold, I made him a witness (ed) to the peoples (le'ummim), a leader (nagid) and commander (metsavveh) for the peoples' (55:4). The Davidic king is not just Israel's ruler but the nations' leader.

sections.heading: The Effectual Word and the Cosmic Exodus (55:6-13)
sections.body: 'Seek YHWH while he may be found (behimmatse'o); call upon him while he is near (beqarvo)' (55:6). The urgency is temporal -- the window of grace is open now. 'Let the wicked forsake his way (darko)... let him return (yashuv) to YHWH, that he may have compassion (yerachamenu) on him, and to our God, for he will abundantly pardon (yarbeh lislo'ach)' (55:7). Then the incomprehensibility passage: 'For my thoughts (machshevotai) are not your thoughts, neither are your ways (darkeikhem) my ways, declares YHWH. For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts' (55:8-9). YHWH's counsel is transcendent -- the divine council's planning exceeds all human calculation. The word guarantee: 'As the rain and the snow come down from heaven and do not return there but water the earth, making it bring forth and sprout... so shall my word (devari) be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty (reqam), but it shall accomplish (asah) that which I purpose (chaphatseti), and shall succeed (hitsliyach) in the thing for which I sent it' (55:10-11). The cosmic exodus: 'The mountains and the hills before you shall break forth into singing (rinnah), and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands (yimcha'u-khaf)' (55:12). Nature itself celebrates the redemption.

isaiah_servant.title: First Servant Song: The Chosen One Commissioned
isaiah_servant.synopsis: The first Servant Song opens with YHWH's own introduction of his servant: 'Behold my servant ('eved), whom I uphold (etomech-bo), my chosen (bechiyr), in whom my soul delights (ratsah)' (42:1). [A] This is commissioning language drawn directly from the divine council: YHWH presents his representative before the assembled heavens. [B] The servant's mandate is the broadest possible -- mishpat (justice/right order) to the nations (goyim) -- the very nations assigned to corrupt elohim at Babel (Deut 32:8). [B] His method is the opposite of every ANE divine warrior: not military conquest but patient, gentle, persistent care for the broken. [A] Matthew 12:18-21 quotes this passage directly and applies it to Jesus, making the NT identification unambiguous.

key_verse.text: Behold my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights; I have put my Spirit upon him; he will bring forth justice to the nations.

hebrew_terms: 'eved (servant -- covenant representative, not slave; one who acts with the master's full authority)
bechiyr (chosen one -- the elect, specifically chosen for a purpose, same root as bachar)
ratsah (delight/pleasure -- the same word used for an accepted sacrifice; YHWH's approval is total)
mishpat (justice/right order -- not merely legal verdict but the restoration of covenant order to creation)
goyim (nations -- the Deut 32:8 nations assigned to corrupt elohim, now the servant's mission field)
qaneh ratsuts (bruised reed -- a reed crushed but not broken; the marginal, broken people)
pishtah kehah (faintly burning wick -- a flicker of hope nearly extinguished)
berit 'am (covenant for the people -- the servant IS the covenant personified, not merely a covenant mediator)
or goyim (light for the nations -- the servant as the means by which YHWH's glory reaches all peoples)
tzedek (righteousness -- the moral-legal ground on which the servant is called and commissioned)
ruach (Spirit -- the divine breath placed on the servant for his mission, echoing Gen 1:2)
isaiah_servant.ane_backdrop: In the ancient Near East, the commissioning of a royal servant (Akkadian: ardu) was a formal ceremony in which the king publicly presented his representative, declared his trust, and delegated authority. The Babylonian royal annals contain numerous scenes where Marduk or another deity 'grasps the hand' of the king, a gesture of election and support -- exactly the language of Isaiah 42:6 ('I will take you by the hand and keep you'). What makes the Servant Songs explosive in their ANE context is the reversal of the divine warrior paradigm: Marduk's agent Nebuchadnezzar brought justice through military conquest, burning cities and deporting populations. YHWH's servant brings mishpat by refusing to break bruised reeds. The contrast is not subtle -- it is the direct claim that YHWH's method of cosmic restoration is categorically different from every other nation's theology.

second_temple.summary: Matthew quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 in full as the fulfillment of Jesus' healing ministry and his command to silence those he healed. The quotation is the longest OT citation in Matthew and is introduced with 'This was to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet Isaiah.'
second_temple.relevance: The NT's most explicit identification of Jesus as the Isaiah 42 Servant. Matthew's insertion here is deliberate -- Jesus' refusal to create public spectacle IS the fulfillment of 'he will not quarrel or cry aloud' (42:2), his method of healing the broken IS the fulfillment of 'a bruised reed he will not break' (42:3).

second_temple.summary: In the Nazareth synagogue, Jesus reads from Isaiah 61 -- a passage that shares the servant's Spirit-anointing and mission to the broken -- and declares 'Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.'
second_temple.relevance: Isaiah 61 draws on the Servant Songs' language deliberately. Jesus' self-identification in Luke 4 positions him within the larger servant tradition that Isaiah 42 inaugurates.

second_temple.summary: The Great Isaiah Scroll from Qumran (1QIsa-a) preserves Isaiah 42 with only minor orthographic variants from the Masoretic text, confirming the textual stability of the passage. The Qumran community read the Servant Songs as eschatological prophecies awaiting fulfillment.
second_temple.relevance: The DSS confirm the antiquity of the Servant Song text and demonstrate that Second Temple Judaism was actively reading these passages messianically before Jesus.

The anointed king declared YHWH's son and given the nations as his inheritance

Isaiah's own divine council commissioning scene -- the servant's is structurally parallel

isaiah_servant.divine_council_note: Isaiah 42:1 is a divine council presentation scene. YHWH speaks to the assembled heavenly court ('Behold my servant') in the same way a king presents his emissary before the royal council before sending him on a critical mission. The Spirit ('ruach) placed on the servant is the divine empowerment for council-delegated work -- the same ruach that hovered over creation (Gen 1:2) and that equipped judges and prophets. The servant's mission to bring mishpat to the goyim is the council's assignment to reverse the Deut 32:8 judgment: what the elohim corrupted over the nations, the servant will restore -- not by displacing them militarily but by being 'a covenant for the people, a light for the nations' (42:6). The servant acts as YHWH's sole authorized agent in the cosmic reclamation project, and his appointment is declared before the divine assembly as a formal, irreversible commission.

sections.heading: The Presentation: YHWH Introduces His Servant (42:1)
sections.body: 'Hen 'avdi etomech-bo, bechiyriy ratsah nafshi' -- 'Behold my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights' (42:1). Every word is freighted with significance. 'Hen' ('behold') is the Hebrew attention marker -- this is a formal announcement. ''Avdi' ('my servant') deploys 'eved in its highest covenantal sense: not a slave who serves under compulsion but the trusted representative who acts with the master's full authority. Moses is called 'eved YHWH (Num 12:7); so is David (2 Sam 7:5). This servant steps into that lineage. 'Etomech-bo' ('I uphold him') -- the verb tamach means to grasp firmly, to support structurally. YHWH holds this servant from beneath; the mission will not fail because YHWH is the foundation. 'Bechiyriy' ('my chosen one') -- from bachar, the vocabulary of divine election. Israel was bachar (Deut 7:6), but now a single individual bears the full weight of that election. 'Ratsah nafshi' ('my soul delights') -- ratsah is the word used when a sacrifice is accepted, when a gift finds full approval. YHWH's delight in this servant is not conditional or partial. 'Natati ruachi 'alav' ('I have put my Spirit upon him') -- the ruach empowers the servant for his commission. And his mandate: 'mishpat la-goyim yotsi' -- 'he will bring forth justice to the nations.' Not to Israel alone. The nations. The Deut 32:8 nations now have a servant coming to them with YHWH's right order.

sections.heading: The Method: Gentle Restoration, Not Conquest (42:2-4)
sections.body: What makes the First Servant Song theologically explosive is its description of HOW the servant accomplishes his mission. 'He will not cry aloud or lift up his voice, or make it heard in the street' (42:2). No triumphalist proclamation. No military fanfare. No conquering hero entering with noise. Every ANE divine warrior -- Marduk, Baal, Ashur -- demonstrated power through loud, visible, violent victory. YHWH's servant works quietly. Then the most remarkable description in the entire passage: 'A bruised reed (qaneh ratsuts) he will not break, and a faintly burning wick (pishtah kehah) he will not quench' (42:3). The qaneh ratsuts is a river reed, the kind used to make flutes and measuring rods. When a reed is cracked it is normally discarded -- it is useless for its intended purpose. The pishtah kehah is a flax wick that has burned down to the point of producing more smoke than light -- normally snuffed out. The servant goes to these: the broken, the barely-alive, the ones who have failed their function. He does not break them. He does not extinguish them. He nurses them back. This is the cosmic reclamation strategy. Not conquest of strong nations but restoration of broken people within those nations. 'He will faithfully bring forth mishpat (justice). He will not grow faint or be discouraged till he has established mishpat in the earth; and the coastlands (iyim) wait for his Torah' (42:4). The coastlands are the far edges of the known world -- and they are waiting for the servant's instruction.

sections.heading: The Commission: Covenant for the People, Light for the Nations (42:5-9)
sections.body: YHWH now speaks directly to the servant in verses 6-7, and the language escalates. 'I am YHWH; I have called you in righteousness (tzedek); I will take you by the hand (ve'etchazek be-yadecha) and keep you' (42:6). 'Taking by the hand' is the formal act of royal installation throughout the ancient Near East. When Marduk 'grasped the hand' of the Babylonian king at the Akitu festival, it legitimized his reign. When YHWH 'takes the hand' of the servant, the commission is absolute. Then the stunning declaration: 'I will give you as a berit 'am (covenant for the people), a light (or) for the nations (goyim)' (42:6). The servant is not merely a covenant mediator. The servant IS the covenant. The covenant is personified in this individual. This is the most extraordinary messianic claim in the entire song: YHWH says the covenant relationship between himself and his people is embodied in this servant. The servant's person IS the bond. His mission: 'to open the eyes (poqeach 'einayim) of the blind, to bring out the prisoners from the dungeon, from the prison those who sit in darkness' (42:7). The language is both literal and cosmic: physical healing of the blind AND liberation of those imprisoned in spiritual darkness under the corrupt elohim. YHWH closes the speech with a sovereignty claim that admits no rival: 'I am YHWH; that is my name; my glory I give to no other, nor my praise to carved idols' (42:8). The servant's mission advances YHWH's glory alone.

isaiah_servant.title: Second Servant Song: Light for the Nations
isaiah_servant.synopsis: The Second Servant Song opens with the servant addressing not Israel but the nations directly: 'Listen to me, O coastlands, and give attention, you peoples from afar' (49:1). [A] The servant now speaks in first person, describing his pre-birth calling, his apparent failure, and his expanded mandate. [B] Verse 4's brutal honesty -- 'I have labored in vain; I have spent my strength for nothing and vanity' -- preserves the raw emotional texture of the servant's own assessment of his mission. [A] The theological climax comes in verse 6: the servant's scope is not merely to restore Israel but to be 'a light for the nations, that my salvation may reach to the end of the earth.' [A] Paul quotes this verse directly in Acts 13:47 as the mandate for Gentile mission.

key_verse.text: He says: 'It is too light a thing that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob and to bring back the preserved of Israel; I will make you as a light for the nations, that my salvation may reach to the end of the earth.'

hebrew_terms: 'iyim (coastlands/islands -- the far reaches of the known world, the most distant nations)
le'om (peoples -- ethnic groups, parallel to goyim; the servant's audience is all humanity)
beten (womb -- the place of pre-birth calling; the servant's identity is named before birth)
charev (sword -- the servant's mouth is made a sharp sword; the word as weapon of justice)
chatsi (polished arrow -- the servant as a prepared, aimed projectile of YHWH's purpose)
yaga' larik (labored in vain -- raw honest confession; toil that appeared to produce nothing)
or goyim (light for the nations -- the servant as YHWH's illuminating agent to all peoples)
yeshu'ati (my salvation -- YHWH's saving work, reaching 'to the end of the earth')
nivzeh nefesh (one deeply despised -- the servant as contemptible, rejected; anticipates Isaiah 53)
me'av lo goy (abhorred by the nation -- the servant will be rejected by Israel before being glorified)
qodesh (holy one -- YHWH as Israel's Holy One who chose the servant; his holiness is the commission's ground)
isaiah_servant.ane_backdrop: The address to 'coastlands and peoples from afar' positions the servant's speech as a royal proclamation to the known world -- a form recognizable from Persian royal edicts (like Cyrus's cylinder) and Babylonian victory inscriptions that addressed all nations as witnesses. The 'polished arrow hidden in a quiver' metaphor (49:2) draws on the well-known image of an archer-king preserving his most lethal weapon for the decisive moment -- the servant is kept in reserve until the appointed time. The language of being 'called from the womb' parallels Jeremiah 1:5 and echoes Egyptian royal inscriptions describing pharaoh's divine election before birth, but the servant's election is not to earthly kingship -- it is to a mission that will reorder all nations. The concept of a despised individual becoming the means of cosmic salvation inverts every ANE honor-shame framework: greatness comes through rejection, not military victory.

second_temple.summary: Paul and Barnabas quote Isaiah 49:6 directly when the Jewish synagogue in Pisidian Antioch rejects their message: 'For so the Lord has commanded us, saying, I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.'
second_temple.relevance: The apostles apply the servant's mandate to their own Gentile mission. The servant's 'light for the nations' is not only Jesus but the community that carries his message -- the ekklesia participates in the servant's cosmic scope.

second_temple.summary: Simeon in the temple holds the infant Jesus and declares him 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to your people Israel' -- a direct echo of Isaiah 49:6.
second_temple.relevance: The earliest Gentile identification of Jesus in the Gospel narrative comes through the Isaiah 49 servant language. Simeon recognizes the infant as the fulfillment of the servant's cosmic mandate.

second_temple.summary: The Qumran Isaiah pesher interprets the servant passages eschatologically, connecting the servant's mission with the end-time restoration of Israel and the judgment of the nations. The community read the 'light for the nations' as a future event associated with the coming anointed figure.
second_temple.relevance: DSS evidence confirms that Second Temple Jewish readers understood Isaiah 49 as pointing to a future individual, not just a corporate Israel, who would execute a cosmic mission to the nations.

The righteous sufferer 'scorned by mankind, despised by the people' -- the servant's rejection language
Paul cites Isaiah 49:6 as the direct mandate for Gentile mission
Simeon's recognition of Jesus as the 'light for the Gentiles' of Isaiah 49:6
